Money Stuff =0A When you deposit money at a bank, you expect the bank to gi=
ve it back to you. =0AThere are two things you might worry about, two sets =
of risks =0A=0A=0A =0A<https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/authors/ARbTQlRLRj=
E/matthew-s-levine=3Fcmpid=3DBBD112524_MONEYSTUFF&utm_medium=3Demail&utm_so=
urce=3Dnewsletter&utm_term=3D241125&utm_campaign=3Dmoneystuff>=0A =0A<https=
://sli.bloomberg.com/click=3Fs=3D825881&stpe=3Ddefault&li=3D11827566&m=3D21=
d6dea82d6b16615cf44233be620525&p=3D11252024>=0A=0A=0A=0ASynapse=0A=0AWhen y=
ou deposit money at a bank, you expect the bank to give it back to you. =0A=

, two sets of risks that might prevent the bank from giving you back your m=
oney. One, which we talk about a lot around here, and which is pretty centr=
al to the history and theory of banking and bank regulation, is that the ba=
nk might lose the money. Banks do not generally just keep your money in the=
 vault. They use it=C2=A0to make loans, so there is risk: The loans might d=
efault, or depositors might all demand their money back at once when=C2=A0t=
he bank does not have a lot of ready cash.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">These kinds of=C2=A0risk are=C2=A0very well understood, there is an e=
xtensive literature about them, they=E2=80=99re in=C2=A0<em>It=E2=80=99s a =
Wonderful Life</em>, and bank capital and liquidity regulation and prudenti=
al supervision are designed to minimize the risk that a bank will lose your=
 money by investing it poorly. And if things do go wrong, there is an exten=
sive system of government backstops =E2=80=94=C2=A0the Federal Reserve as a=
 lender of last resort, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation=E2=80=99s=
 guarantee of bank deposits, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0to make sure that deposito=
rs will get their money anyway.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The other r=
isk, which we talk about less, and which is sort of less intellectually int=
eresting, is that the bank might lose=C2=A0<em>track=C2=A0</em>of the money=
. You might go to the bank and deposit $100, and the bank might write down=
 =E2=80=9C$100=E2=80=9D next to your name in its notebook,=C2=A0and then it=
 might spill coffee on the notebook and be unable to read the entry and for=
get that it owes you the $100. And then you might come back to the bank in =
a week and ask for your $100 back and the bank might say =E2=80=9Cwho are y=
ou=3F what $100=3F=E2=80=9D And the bank might be totally solvent and have =
invested your $100 in very safe things, but it won=E2=80=99t give it back t=
o you because it doesn=E2=80=99t have a record of you.</p><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">This risk is=C2=A0<em>also </em><a href=3D"https://links.messag=

=80=99s partner banks in custodial =E2=80=9CFBO=E2=80=9D accounts, approxim=
